# The Culdesac: Website Overview

## Layout & Structure
This is a clean, minimalist personal blog built around a centered container with a maximum width of 720px. The layout includes a header with navigation, main content area, and footer—all organized vertically for easy reading.

## Color Scheme & Typography
The design employs a sophisticated palette: white background, dark charcoal text (#1a1a1a), muted gray accents (#888), and a warm terracotta accent color (#C67D5B). The site uses system fonts (Apple System, Segoe UI, Roboto) for modern, accessible typography.

## Key Sections

**Header:** Features the site title "The Culdesac" alongside navigation links to Blog and About pages.

**Bio Section:** Introduces Shiyan Koh as a co-founder at Hustle Fund, with a brief description: "Writing about startups, communities, parenting, and discovery." Includes links to professional profiles (Hustle Fund, LinkedIn, X).

**Blog Feed:** Displays 24+ articles in reverse chronological order, from February 2026 back to January 2018. Each post includes title, date, and excerpt.

**Footer:** Contains copyright information and a newsletter subscription link via Paragraph.

## Notable Features
The design emphasizes content over decoration, with responsive breakpoints for mobile devices. The terracotta accent color creates visual hierarchy throughout clickable elements. There's a distinct focus on readable prose and community-building themes reflected in post titles like "On building communities" and "Communities and the Cul-de-sac."

Dependencies to Add ```bash npm install gray-matter remark remark-html ``` No type packages needed: `gray-matter` ships its own types, and `remark`/`remark-html` are typed via the unified ecosystem. `@types/node` is already a dev dependency (present in `package.json`). You will need it for `fs` and `path` usage in `src/lib/posts.ts` - it is already there, so no action needed. One important note: do NOT add `rehype-sanitize` or any runtime sanitizer. Since you control 100% of the markdown content in `content/posts/`, remark-html's default output is fine. Adding sanitizers creates unnecessary complexity. The dependency set stays intentionally minimal: `gray-matter` + `remark` + `remark-html`. This produces the full `gray-matter` frontmatter parsing and remark's HTML conversion in ~3 packages. --- ### 3. Markdown Blog Post System **Frontmatter schema** for every file in `content/posts/`: ```markdown --- title: "The Founder as Director" date: "2026-01-15" excerpt: "Why the next generation of founders won't write code or copy — they'll direct agents." slug: "founder-as-director" published: true --- Post body in standard markdown here. ``` Field rules: - `slug` must match the filename exactly (e.g. `founder-as-director.md` has `slug: "founder-as-director"`) - `date` is ISO 8601 string (`YYYY-MM-DD`), sorted descending for display - `published: false` drafts exist in `content/posts/` but are filtered out at build time - `excerpt` is manually written (65-120 chars), not auto-generated from body **Data flow for static export:** The critical constraint is `output: 'export'` in `next.config.ts`. This means: - No `getServerSideProps`, no API routes, no Node.js runtime at request time - All data must be fetched at build time using `generateStaticParams` + direct `fs` calls in Server Components The data flow works as follows: ``` content/posts/*.md ↓ (build time only) src/lib/posts.ts ← reads with fs.readdirSync / fs.readFileSync ↓ gray-matter ← parses YAML frontmatter → { data, content } ↓ remark → remark-html ← converts markdown body → HTML string ↓ Server Components (blog/page.tsx, blog/[slug]/page.tsx, page.tsx) ↓ Static HTML at build → out/ directory ``` **`src/lib/posts.ts` - full design:** ```typescript // Types export interface PostMeta { slug: string; title: string; date: string; excerpt: string; published: boolean; } export interface Post extends PostMeta { contentHtml: string; } // Three exported functions: // getAllPostMetas() → PostMeta[] // Used by: home page (takes first 3), blog list page // Does: readdirSync content/posts/, reads each file, // runs gray-matter on frontmatter only, skips published:false, // sorts by date descending // getPostBySlug(slug: string) → Post // Used by: blog/[slug]/page.tsx // Does: reads single file, gray-matter parse, remark → remark-html, // returns full Post with contentHtml // getAllSlugs() → string[] // Used by: generateStaticParams in blog/[slug]/page.tsx // Does: readdirSync, strips .md extension, returns slug array ``` The `posts.ts` file uses `process.cwd()` to construct the absolute path to `content/posts/`. This is safe at build time in Next.js. The critical detail: these functions use synchronous Node.js fs APIs (`readdirSync`, `readFileSync`). This is fine because they only run at build time under static export - never at request time. --- ### 4. Pixel Art Icon System **Core approach:** Each icon is a React component that renders a `

Current Bets

``` This becomes: ```tsx

``` Apply the same pattern to "Previous" (PixelExperienceIcon) and "Philosophy" (PixelPhilosophyIcon). **Change B - Add "Writing" section** between Philosophy and Socials. The section shows the latest 3 posts fetched at build time. This requires converting `Home` from a client component to a Server Component (it currently has no `'use client'` directive, so it already IS a Server Component - no change needed). ```tsx // At the top of page.tsx, import: import { getAllPostMetas } from '@/lib/posts'; import { PixelBlogIcon } from '@/components/icons/PixelBlogIcon'; import Link from 'next/link'; // Inside the async component: const recentPosts = (await getAllPostMetas()).slice(0, 3); // Wait - getAllPostMetas uses sync fs, so no await needed. // But wrapping Home() as async is fine for Server Components. ``` The Writing section layout: ```tsx

The `T00:00:00` suffix prevents UTC timezone drift from shifting the date by one day. **Section order after changes:** 1. Header (bio) - no pixel icon, stays clean 2. Current Bets - adds PixelBetsIcon 3. Previous (Experience) - adds PixelExperienceIcon 4. Philosophy - adds PixelPhilosophyIcon 5. Writing (NEW) - PixelBlogIcon + 3 recent posts 6. Socials (unchanged) 7. Footer (unchanged) --- ### 6. Blog List Page (`/blog`) `src/app/blog/page.tsx`: ```tsx import { getAllPostMetas } from '@/lib/posts'; import Link from 'next/link'; export const metadata = { title: 'Writing — Can Erden', description: 'Essays on AI-native founding, growth, and strategy.', }; export default function BlogPage() { const posts = getAllPostMetas(); // sync, no await needed return (
← Can Erden

Writing

Essays on AI-native founding, growth, and strategy.

{posts.map((post) => (

{post.title}

{formatDate(post.date)}

{post.excerpt}

))}

); } ``` Layout mirrors the existing home page's section/spacing style exactly. The chronological list matches the shiyankoh.com inspiration. No pagination needed until you have 20+ posts. --- ### 7. Individual Post Page (`/blog/[slug]`) `src/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx`: ```tsx import { getPostBySlug, getAllSlugs } from '@/lib/posts'; import Link from 'next/link'; import { notFound } from 'next/navigation'; // Required for static export: generates all slugs at build time export function generateStaticParams() { return getAllSlugs().map((slug) => ({ slug })); } // Dynamic metadata per post export async function generateMetadata({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { const post = getPostBySlug(params.slug); if (!post) return {}; return { title: `${post.title} — Can Erden`, description: post.excerpt, }; } export default function PostPage({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { const post = getPostBySlug(params.slug); if (!post) notFound(); return (
← Writing

{post.title}

{formatDate(post.date)}

← Back to writing
© 2026 Can Erden
); } ``` **Prose styling for `.prose-content` in `globals.css`:** Since you are using Tailwind v4 (not v3), the `@tailwindcss/typography` plugin is not yet stable for v4. Instead, define prose styles manually in `globals.css`: ```css .prose-content { font-size: 0.9375rem; /* 15px - slightly smaller than base */ line-height: 1.75; color: var(--muted); } .prose-content p { margin-bottom: 1.25rem; } .prose-content h2 { font-size: 1.1rem; font-weight: 700; color: #000; margin: 2rem 0 0.75rem; } .prose-content h3 { font-size: 1rem; font-weight: 600; color: #000; margin: 1.5rem 0 0.5rem; } .prose-content a { color: #000; text-decoration: underline; text-underline-offset: 4px; } .prose-content ul { list-style: disc; padding-left: 1.5rem; margin-bottom: 1.25rem; } .prose-content ol { list-style: decimal; padding-left: 1.5rem; margin-bottom: 1.25rem; } .prose-content li { margin-bottom: 0.375rem; } .prose-content blockquote { border-left: 2px solid #e5e5e5; padding-left: 1rem; color: #737373; margin: 1.5rem 0; } .prose-content code { font-size: 0.8em; background: #f5f5f5; padding: 0.1em 0.3em; border-radius: 3px; } .prose-content pre { background: #f5f5f5; padding: 1rem; border-radius: 6px; overflow-x: auto; margin-bottom: 1.25rem; } .prose-content pre code { background: none; padding: 0; } .prose-content strong { color: #000; font-weight: 600; } ``` This replaces `@tailwindcss/typography` entirely and gives you full control over the reading aesthetic. --- ### 8. Tricky Parts and Gotchas **Gotcha 1: `output: 'export'` and `generateStaticParams` is non-negotiable.** Without `generateStaticParams` in `blog/[slug]/page.tsx`, the static build will fail with a "Dynamic server usage" error because Next.js cannot enumerate the slugs. The function must return an array of all possible `{ slug }` objects. `getAllSlugs()` provides this from the filesystem at build time. **Gotcha 2: `params` is now a Promise in Next.js 15+.** In Next.js 15 and later (your version is 16.1.6), the `params` prop in dynamic route pages is a `Promise`. The correct signature is: ```tsx export default async function PostPage({ params, }: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }>; }) { const { slug } = await params; const post = getPostBySlug(slug); // ... } ``` Same applies to `generateMetadata`: ```tsx export async function generateMetadata({ params, }: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }>; }) { const { slug } = await params; // ... } ``` This is a breaking change from Next.js 14 behavior. Missing the `await` will cause TypeScript errors or subtle runtime bugs. **Gotcha 3: `process.cwd()` path construction in `posts.ts`.** The posts directory path must be constructed as: ```typescript import path from 'path'; const postsDirectory = path.join(process.cwd(), 'content', 'posts'); ``` Do NOT use `__dirname` (it will point to `.next/` in build output). Do NOT use relative paths. `process.cwd()` always resolves to the project root during the Next.js build, which is what you want. **Gotcha 4: Date parsing timezone drift.** JavaScript's `new Date('2026-01-15')` (date-only ISO string) is parsed as UTC midnight, which means in timezones west of UTC it displays as the previous day. Always append `T00:00:00` when constructing Date objects from frontmatter date strings: ```typescript const formatDate = (dateStr: string) => new Date(dateStr + 'T00:00:00').toLocaleDateString('en-US', { year: 'numeric', month: 'short', }); ``` **Gotcha 5: `reactCompiler: true` in `next.config.ts`.** You have the React Compiler enabled. The compiler automatically memoizes components. Your pixel icon components need to be pure functions (no side effects, deterministic output given props) — which they will be, since they are static SVG renderers. No action needed, just be aware. **Gotcha 6: The font variable name mismatch.** In `layout.tsx`, the Inter font is assigned to `variable: "--font-geist-sans"` (legacy naming from when the project was scaffolded with Geist). The `body` in `globals.css` references `var(--font-geist-sans)`. This works fine today and should not be changed — touching it would require updating both files simultaneously and could break the font stack. **Gotcha 7: `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` and XSS.** You are using `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` to render `contentHtml` from remark-html. Since the content comes exclusively from your own local `content/posts/` directory (not from user input or a CMS), this is completely safe. If you ever add a CMS or user-generated content, revisit this with `rehype-sanitize`. **Gotcha 8: pixel icons and `image-rendering: pixelated`.** SVG icons with integer pixel grids look sharp at their native resolution but may appear blurry when the browser scales them to non-integer sizes. Complete File Structure **New files to create:** ``` /Users/canerden/personal-website/ ├── content/ │ └── posts/ │ ├── founder-as-director.md ← first post (sample) │ └── why-agentic-ai-changes-gtm.md ← second post (sample) ├── src/ │ ├── app/ │ │ └── blog/ │ │ ├── page.tsx ← /blog list page │ │ └── [slug]/ │ │ └── page.tsx ← /blog/[slug] post page │ ├── components/ │ │ └── icons/ │ │ ├── PixelBlogIcon.tsx │ │ ├── PixelBetsIcon.tsx │ │ ├── PixelExperienceIcon.tsx │ │ ├── PixelPhilosophyIcon.tsx │ │ └── PixelArrowIcon.tsx │ └── lib/ │ └── posts.ts ← all markdown parsing utilities ``` **Existing files to modify:** ``` /Users/canerden/personal-website/ ├── src/app/page.tsx ← add blog preview section + pixel icons to headers ├── src/app/globals.css ← add pixel-art font-smoothing override + prose styles ├── package.json ← add gray-matter, remark, remark-html, @types/node ``` --- ### 2. Dependencies to Add ```bash npm install gray-matter remark remark-html ``` No type packages needed: `gray-matter` ships its own types, and `remark`/`remark-html` are typed via the unified ecosystem. `@types/node` is already a dev dependency (present in `package.json`). You will need it for `fs` and `path` usage in `src/lib/posts.ts` - it is already there, so no action needed. One important note: do NOT add `rehype-sanitize` or any runtime sanitizer. Since you control 100% of the markdown content in `content/posts/`, remark-html's default output is fine. Adding sanitizers creates unnecessary complexity. The dependency set stays intentionally minimal: `gray-matter` + `remark` + `remark-html`. This produces the full `gray-matter` frontmatter parsing and remark's HTML conversion in ~3 packages. --- ### 3. Markdown Blog Post System **Frontmatter schema** for every file in `content/posts/`: ```markdown --- title: "The Founder as Director" date: "2026-01-15" excerpt: "Why the next generation of founders won't write code or copy — they'll direct agents." slug: "founder-as-director" published: true --- Post body in standard markdown here. ``` Field rules: - `slug` must match the filename exactly (e.g. `founder-as-director.md` has `slug: "founder-as-director"`) - `date` is ISO 8601 string (`YYYY-MM-DD`), sorted descending for display - `published: false` drafts exist in `content/posts/` but are filtered out at build time - `excerpt` is manually written (65-120 chars), not auto-generated from body **Data flow for static export:** The critical constraint is `output: 'export'` in `next.config.ts`. This means: - No `getServerSideProps`, no API routes, no Node.js runtime at request time - All data must be fetched at build time using `generateStaticParams` + direct `fs` calls in Server Components The data flow works as follows: ``` content/posts/*.md ↓ (build time only) src/lib/posts.ts ← reads with fs.readdirSync / fs.readFileSync ↓ gray-matter ← parses YAML frontmatter → { data, content } ↓ remark → remark-html ← converts markdown body → HTML string ↓ Server Components (blog/page.tsx, blog/[slug]/page.tsx, page.tsx) ↓ Static HTML at build → out/ directory ``` **`src/lib/posts.ts` - full design:** ```typescript // Types export interface PostMeta { slug: string; title: string; date: string; excerpt: string; published: boolean; } export interface Post extends PostMeta { contentHtml: string; } // Three exported functions: // getAllPostMetas() → PostMeta[] // Used by: home page (takes first 3), blog list page // Does: readdirSync content/posts/, reads each file, // runs gray-matter on frontmatter only, skips published:false, // sorts by date descending // getPostBySlug(slug: string) → Post // Used by: blog/[slug]/page.tsx // Does: reads single file, gray-matter parse, remark → remark-html, // returns full Post with contentHtml // getAllSlugs() → string[] // Used by: generateStaticParams in blog/[slug]/page.tsx // Does: readdirSync, strips .md extension, returns slug array ``` The `posts.ts` file uses `process.cwd()` to construct the absolute path to `content/posts/`. This is safe at build time in Next.js. The critical detail: these functions use synchronous Node.js fs APIs (`readdirSync`, `readFileSync`). This is fine because they only run at build time under static export - never at request time. --- ### 4. Pixel Art Icon System **Core approach:** Each icon is a React component that renders a `

Current Bets

``` This becomes: ```tsx

Current Bets

``` Apply the same pattern to "Previous" (PixelExperienceIcon) and "Philosophy" (PixelPhilosophyIcon). **Change B - Add "Writing" section** between Philosophy and Socials. The section shows the latest 3 posts fetched at build time. This requires converting `Home` from a client component to a Server Component (it currently has no `'use client'` directive, so it already IS a Server Component - no change needed). ```tsx // At the top of page.tsx, import: import { getAllPostMetas } from '@/lib/posts'; import { PixelBlogIcon } from '@/components/icons/PixelBlogIcon'; import Link from 'next/link'; // Inside the async component: const recentPosts = (await getAllPostMetas()).slice(0, 3); // Wait - getAllPostMetas uses sync fs, so no await needed. // But wrapping Home() as async is fine for Server Components. ``` The Writing section layout: ```tsx

Writing

All posts →
{recentPosts.map((post) => (

{post.title}

{formatDate(post.date)}

{post.excerpt}

))}
``` `formatDate` is a small helper: `new Date(date + 'T00:00:00').toLocaleDateString('en-US', { year: 'numeric', month: 'short' })` — outputs "Jan 2026". The `T00:00:00` suffix prevents UTC timezone drift from shifting the date by one day. **Section order after changes:** 1. Header (bio) - no pixel icon, stays clean 2. Current Bets - adds PixelBetsIcon 3. Previous (Experience) - adds PixelExperienceIcon 4. Philosophy - adds PixelPhilosophyIcon 5. Writing (NEW) - PixelBlogIcon + 3 recent posts 6. Socials (unchanged) 7. Footer (unchanged) --- ### 6. Blog List Page (`/blog`) `src/app/blog/page.tsx`: ```tsx import { getAllPostMetas } from '@/lib/posts'; import Link from 'next/link'; export const metadata = { title: 'Writing — Can Erden', description: 'Essays on AI-native founding, growth, and strategy.', }; export default function BlogPage() { const posts = getAllPostMetas(); // sync, no await needed return (
← Can Erden

Writing

Essays on AI-native founding, growth, and strategy.

{posts.map((post) => (

{post.title}

{formatDate(post.date)}

{post.excerpt}

))}
© 2026 Can Erden
); } ``` Layout mirrors the existing home page's section/spacing style exactly. The chronological list matches the shiyankoh.com inspiration. No pagination needed until you have 20+ posts. --- ### 7. Individual Post Page (`/blog/[slug]`) `src/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx`: ```tsx import { getPostBySlug, getAllSlugs } from '@/lib/posts'; import Link from 'next/link'; import { notFound } from 'next/navigation'; // Required for static export: generates all slugs at build time export function generateStaticParams() { return getAllSlugs().map((slug) => ({ slug })); } // Dynamic metadata per post export async function generateMetadata({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { const post = getPostBySlug(params.slug); if (!post) return {}; return { title: `${post.title} — Can Erden`, description: post.excerpt, }; } export default function PostPage({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { const post = getPostBySlug(params.slug); if (!post) notFound(); return (
← Writing

{post.title}

{formatDate(post.date)}

← Back to writing
© 2026 Can Erden
); } ``` **Prose styling for `.prose-content` in `globals.css`:** Since you are using Tailwind v4 (not v3), the `@tailwindcss/typography` plugin is not yet stable for v4. Instead, define prose styles manually in `globals.css`: ```css .prose-content { font-size: 0.9375rem; /* 15px - slightly smaller than base */ line-height: 1.75; color: var(--muted); } .prose-content p { margin-bottom: 1.25rem; } .prose-content h2 { font-size: 1.1rem; font-weight: 700; color: #000; margin: 2rem 0 0.75rem; } .prose-content h3 { font-size: 1rem; font-weight: 600; color: #000; margin: 1.5rem 0 0.5rem; } .prose-content a { color: #000; text-decoration: underline; text-underline-offset: 4px; } .prose-content ul { list-style: disc; padding-left: 1.5rem; margin-bottom: 1.25rem; } .prose-content ol { list-style: decimal; padding-left: 1.5rem; margin-bottom: 1.25rem; } .prose-content li { margin-bottom: 0.375rem; } .prose-content blockquote { border-left: 2px solid #e5e5e5; padding-left: 1rem; color: #737373; margin: 1.5rem 0; } .prose-content code { font-size: 0.8em; background: #f5f5f5; padding: 0.1em 0.3em; border-radius: 3px; } .prose-content pre { background: #f5f5f5; padding: 1rem; border-radius: 6px; overflow-x: auto; margin-bottom: 1.25rem; } .prose-content pre code { background: none; padding: 0; } .prose-content strong { color: #000; font-weight: 600; } ``` This replaces `@tailwindcss/typography` entirely and gives you full control over the reading aesthetic. --- ### 8. Tricky Parts and Gotchas **Gotcha 1: `output: 'export'` and `generateStaticParams` is non-negotiable.** Without `generateStaticParams` in `blog/[slug]/page.tsx`, the static build will fail with a "Dynamic server usage" error because Next.js cannot enumerate the slugs. The function must return an array of all possible `{ slug }` objects. `getAllSlugs()` provides this from the filesystem at build time. **Gotcha 2: `params` is now a Promise in Next.js 15+.** In Next.js 15 and later (your version is 16.1.6), the `params` prop in dynamic route pages is a `Promise`. The correct signature is: ```tsx export default async function PostPage({ params, }: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }>; }) { const { slug } = await params; const post = getPostBySlug(slug); // ... } ``` Same applies to `generateMetadata`: ```tsx export async function generateMetadata({ params, }: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }>; }) { const { slug } = await params; // ... } ``` This is a breaking change from Next.js 14 behavior. Missing the `await` will cause TypeScript errors or subtle runtime bugs. **Gotcha 3: `process.cwd()` path construction in `posts.ts`.** The posts directory path must be constructed as: ```typescript import path from 'path'; const postsDirectory = path.join(process.cwd(), 'content', 'posts'); ``` Do NOT use `__dirname` (it will point to `.next/` in build output). Do NOT use relative paths. `process.cwd()` always resolves to the project root during the Next.js build, which is what you want. **Gotcha 4: Date parsing timezone drift.** JavaScript's `new Date('2026-01-15')` (date-only ISO string) is parsed as UTC midnight, which means in timezones west of UTC it displays as the previous day. Always append `T00:00:00` when constructing Date objects from frontmatter date strings: ```typescript const formatDate = (dateStr: string) => new Date(dateStr + 'T00:00:00').toLocaleDateString('en-US', { year: 'numeric', month: 'short', }); ``` **Gotcha 5: `reactCompiler: true` in `next.config.ts`.** You have the React Compiler enabled. The compiler automatically memoizes components. Your pixel icon components need to be pure functions (no side effects, deterministic output given props) — which they will be, since they are static SVG renderers. No action needed, just be aware. **Gotcha 6: The font variable name mismatch.** In `layout.tsx`, the Inter font is assigned to `variable: "--font-geist-sans"` (legacy naming from when the project was scaffolded with Geist). The `body` in `globals.css` references `var(--font-geist-sans)`. This works fine today and should not be changed — touching it would require updating both files simultaneously and could break the font stack. **Gotcha 7: `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` and XSS.** You are using `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` to render `contentHtml` from remark-html. Since the content comes exclusively from your own local `content/posts/` directory (not from user input or a CMS), this is completely safe. If you ever add a CMS or user-generated content, revisit this with `rehype-sanitize`. **Gotcha 8: pixel icons and `image-rendering: pixelated`.** SVG icons with integer pixel grids look sharp at their native resolution but may appear blurry when the browser scales them to non-integer sizes. Add this to `globals.css`: ```css .pixel-icon { image-rendering: pixelated; image-rendering: crisp-edges; /* Firefox fallback */ } ``` Apply `className="pixel-icon"` to the `

# Personal Website: Blog + Pixel Art Upgrade

## Context
You want a personal website with a blog ("thoughts"), intro, socials, and startups — inspired by shiyankoh.com's clean minimal layout, but differentiated by pixel art icons. You already have a working Next.js 16 + React 19 + Tailwind v4 site at `/Users/canerden/personal-website` with your bio, projects, and experience. This plan extends it with a blog and pixel icon design system.

---

## What Gets Built

### 1. Blog Infrastructure
- `content/posts/*.md` — markdown files with YAML frontmatter
- `src/lib/posts.ts` — data layer (reads files, parses frontmatter, converts markdown → HTML)
- `src/app/blog/page.tsx` — full post list
- `src/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` — individual post

### 2. Pixel Art Icon Components
Five 8x8 pixel icons rendered as inline SVGs (using `<rect>` elements on a pixel grid):
- `PixelBlogIcon` — pen/document (for "Writing" section)
- `PixelBetsIcon` — rocket (for "Current Bets")
- `PixelExperienceIcon` — briefcase (for "Previous")
- `PixelPhilosophyIcon` — lightbulb (for "Philosophy")
- `PixelArrowIcon` — diagonal arrow (replaces lucide ArrowUpRight on links)

All icons use `currentColor` so they automatically inherit text color, and get `image-rendering: pixelated` for crispness.

### 3. Updated Home Page
- Section headers get inline pixel icons (12px, beside the heading text)
- New "Writing" section added before Socials — shows latest 3 posts with title, date, excerpt
- "All posts →" link to `/blog`

---

## File Structure

**New files:**
```
content/posts/
  founder-as-director.md
  why-agentic-ai-changes-gtm.md
src/
  lib/
    posts.ts
  components/
    icons/
      PixelBlogIcon.tsx
      PixelBetsIcon.tsx
      PixelExperienceIcon.tsx
      PixelPhilosophyIcon.tsx
      PixelArrowIcon.tsx
  app/
    blog/
      page.tsx
      [slug]/
        page.tsx
```

**Modified files:**
```
src/app/page.tsx          — add Writing section + pixel icons to headers
src/app/globals.css       — add .prose-content styles + .pixel-icon CSS
```

---

## Key Technical Details

### Dependencies to add
```bash
npm install gray-matter remark remark-html
```

### Post frontmatter schema
```markdown
---
title: "The Founder as Director"
date: "2026-01-15"
excerpt: "Why founders won't write code — they'll direct agents."
slug: "founder-as-director"
published: true
---
```

### Data flow (all at build time, compatible with `output: 'export'`)
`content/posts/*.md` → `fs.readFileSync` → `gray-matter` (frontmatter) → `remark-html` (body) → Server Components → static HTML in `out/`

### Critical: Next.js 16 async params
Dynamic route `params` is a Promise in Next.js 15+:
```tsx
export default async function PostPage({
  params,
}: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) {
  const { slug } = await params;
```

### Blog post prose styles
Since `@tailwindcss/typography` isn't stable for Tailwind v4, prose styles are added manually as `.prose-content` in `globals.css` (h2, p, ul, blockquote, code, etc.).

---

## Verification
1. `npm install` — confirm gray-matter, remark, remark-html added
2. `npm run dev` — home page shows pixel icons in section headers + Writing section with sample posts
3. Visit `/blog` — all posts listed chronologically
4. Visit `/blog/founder-as-director` — full post renders with prose styling
5. `npm run build` — confirm `out/blog/` and `out/blog/founder-as-director/index.html` exist (static export works)

---
title: "The Founder as Director"
date: "2026-02-10"
excerpt: "Why the next generation of founders won't write code or copy — they'll direct agents and ship at a pace that was impossible before."
slug: "founder-as-director"
published: true
---

There's a mental model I keep coming back to: the founder as a film director.

A director doesn't operate the camera, design the costumes, or edit the footage. They hold the vision, make the calls, and orchestrate specialists toward a shared outcome. They're not less capable than the people they direct — they're operating at a different level of abstraction.

I think this is the right frame for what's happening to founders right now.

## The old model is breaking

For the past decade, "technical co-founder" was shorthand for "the person who can build the thing." Non-technical founders needed to either learn to code, find a co-founder who could, or raise enough money to hire one.

That bottleneck is collapsing.

Not because coding got easier — it got faster and more agentic. You don't need to write the implementation; you need to know what to build, why, and whether what's been built is right. That's a different skill set.

## What the director model actually looks like

In practice, it means:

- Writing clear, testable requirements instead of code
- Reviewing agent output for correctness and fit, not just functionality
- Shipping feedback loops so AI can iterate rather than asking humans to
- Keeping the strategic thread while agents handle execution sprints

The founders I see winning with this model aren't the ones who know the most about AI. They're the ones with the clearest sense of what they're building and why — and enough taste to recognize when something is wrong.

## Taste is the new bottleneck

When execution becomes cheap and fast, judgment becomes the constraint. What's the right product? The right market? The right sequence of features?

Those questions were always the hard ones. They're just more visibly the hard ones now.

The best directors aren't just technically competent — they have a point of view. They know what the scene needs to feel like before they've shot it. The best AI-native founders are developing the same thing: a conviction about what they're building that survives the ambiguity of early-stage work.

That's what I'm trying to build toward with Habitat and Habitat Skills — not just faster tools, but a way of working that makes founders more themselves, not less.

input.content:
---
title: "Why Communities Beat Solo Building"
date: "2026-01-20"
excerpt: "Solo building is seductive. You move fast, make your own calls, and don't need to manage anyone. But the ceiling is low — and it's lower than most people realize."
slug: "why-communities-beat-solo-building"
published: true
---

Solo building is seductive. You move fast, make your own calls, and don't need to manage anyone. But the ceiling is low — and it's lower than most people realize.

The best version of any startup I've seen wasn't built alone. It was built in a context: a room, a cohort, a Discord server, a group of people who cared about the same problem and were honest enough to tell each other when something wasn't working.

## What a community actually provides

Most people think community is about motivation — someone to cheer you on when you're stuck. That matters, but it's not the main thing.

The main thing is **calibration**.

When you build in isolation, your feedback loop is slow and often skewed. You're the most knowledgeable person about your product, which makes you the worst judge of it. You optimize for things you understand rather than things your users need.

A community of peers — people at roughly the same stage, solving different problems — gives you a fast, honest feedback loop. Not investors, not mentors, not your friends. Peers who have skin in the game of figuring this out alongside you.

## What we learned running hackathons

At Habitat, we've run community hackathons across three cities. The pattern is consistent: the teams that ship the best MVPs in a weekend aren't the ones with the strongest individual builders. They're the ones who talked to other teams, borrowed framings, challenged each other's assumptions.

Cross-pollination is underrated. A founder working on B2B HR software borrowing a UX pattern from someone building a consumer app. A technical founder learning how to pitch by watching a non-technical founder do it well.

These things don't happen when you build alone.

## The loneliness tax

There's also a cost to solo building that doesn't show up in your metrics: it's exhausting in a way that's hard to name.

Startups are full of moments where you don't know if you're making progress. Having people around who are in the same fog — not out of it, not above it — makes it possible to keep going.

That's not soft. That's a real variable in whether you ship or give up.

## The counter-argument

The obvious objection is that communities can become echo chambers. Everyone validates everyone. Nobody tells you the hard truth.

This is real. A bad community is worse than no community. The work is building one where honesty is the norm — where someone saying "this doesn't make sense to me" is a gift, not an attack.

We're still figuring out how to make that happen consistently at Habitat. But the answer isn't to opt out of community. It's to build better ones.

Everything built cleanly. Here's what was done:

---

**Done.** All changes are in `/Users/canerden/personal-website`. Run `npm run dev` to preview.

## What was built

**Blog system**
- `content/posts/*.md` — drop new markdown files here to add posts; frontmatter fields: `title`, `date`, `excerpt`, `slug`, `published`
- `/blog` — full post list, reverse chronological
- `/blog/[slug]` — individual post pages with prose styling
- `src/lib/posts.ts` — data layer (reads files at build time, compatible with `output: 'export'`)

**Pixel art icons** (5 components in `src/components/icons/`)
- Rocket `PixelBetsIcon` → "Current Bets" header
- Briefcase `PixelExperienceIcon` → "Previous" header
- Lightbulb `PixelPhilosophyIcon` → "Philosophy" header
- Pen `PixelBlogIcon` → "Writing" header + blog pages
- Diagonal arrow `PixelArrowIcon` → project links (replaces lucide ArrowUpRight)

All icons use `currentColor` + `image-rendering: pixelated` for crispness.

**Home page** — new "Writing" section before Socials showing your 3 latest posts, with an "All posts →" link.

**To add a new post:** create a `.md` file in `content/posts/` with the frontmatter schema, rebuild. That's it.

# Personal Website: Blog + Pixel Art Upgrade

## Context
You want a personal website with a blog ("thoughts"), intro, socials, and startups — inspired by shiyankoh.com's clean minimal layout, but differentiated by pixel art icons. You already have a working Next.js 16 + React 19 + Tailwind v4 site at `/Users/canerden/personal-website` with your bio, projects, and experience. This plan extends it with a blog and pixel icon design system.

---

## What Gets Built

### 1. Blog Infrastructure
- `content/posts/*.md` — markdown files with YAML frontmatter
- `src/lib/posts.ts` — data layer (reads files, parses frontmatter, converts markdown → HTML)
- `src/app/blog/page.tsx` — full post list
- `src/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` — individual post

### 2. Pixel Art Icon Components
Five 8x8 pixel icons rendered as inline SVGs (using `<rect>` elements on a pixel grid):
- `PixelBlogIcon` — pen/document (for "Writing" section)
- `PixelBetsIcon` — rocket (for "Current Bets")
- `PixelExperienceIcon` — briefcase (for "Previous")
- `PixelPhilosophyIcon` — lightbulb (for "Philosophy")
- `PixelArrowIcon` — diagonal arrow (replaces lucide ArrowUpRight on links)

All icons use `currentColor` so they automatically inherit text color, and get `image-rendering: pixelated` for crispness.

### 3. Updated Home Page
- Section headers get inline pixel icons (12px, beside the heading text)
- New "Writing" section added before Socials — shows latest 3 posts with title, date, excerpt
- "All posts →" link to `/blog`

---

## File Structure

**New files:**
```
content/posts/
  founder-as-director.md
  why-agentic-ai-changes-gtm.md
src/
  lib/
    posts.ts
  components/
    icons/
      PixelBlogIcon.tsx
      PixelBetsIcon.tsx
      PixelExperienceIcon.tsx
      PixelPhilosophyIcon.tsx
      PixelArrowIcon.tsx
  app/
    blog/
      page.tsx
      [slug]/
        page.tsx
```

**Modified files:**
```
src/app/page.tsx          — add Writing section + pixel icons to headers
src/app/globals.css       — add .prose-content styles + .pixel-icon CSS
```

---

## Key Technical Details

### Dependencies to add
```bash
npm install gray-matter remark remark-html
```

### Post frontmatter schema
```markdown
---
title: "The Founder as Director"
date: "2026-01-15"
excerpt: "Why founders won't write code — they'll direct agents."
slug: "founder-as-director"
published: true
---
```

### Data flow (all at build time, compatible with `output: 'export'`)
`content/posts/*.md` → `fs.readFileSync` → `gray-matter` (frontmatter) → `remark-html` (body) → Server Components → static HTML in `out/`

### Critical: Next.js 16 async params
Dynamic route `params` is a Promise in Next.js 15+:
```tsx
export default async function PostPage({
  params,
}: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) {
  const { slug } = await params;
```

### Blog post prose styles
Since `@tailwindcss/typography` isn't stable for Tailwind v4, prose styles are added manually as `.prose-content` in `globals.css` (h2, p, ul, blockquote, code, etc.).

---

## Verification
1. `npm install` — confirm gray-matter, remark, remark-html added
2. `npm run dev` — home page shows pixel icons in section headers + Writing section with sample posts
3. Visit `/blog` — all posts listed chronologically
4. Visit `/blog/founder-as-director` — full post renders with prose styling
5. `npm run build` — confirm `out/blog/` and `out/blog/founder-as-director/index.html` exist (static export works)

# Personal Website: About Page + Navbar + Homepage Simplification

## Context
Homepage currently shows everything (bio, current bets, experience, philosophy, writing, socials). The goal is to strip the homepage to the essentials — 1-sentence bio, philosophy, writing — and move the full CV (bets, experience, socials) to a new `/about` page. A small minimal navbar ties all pages together.

---

## What Gets Built

### 1. Navbar (`src/components/Navbar.tsx`)
- Client Component (`'use client'`) so `usePathname()` can highlight the active link
- Layout: `Can Erden` name on the left, `Home · About · Blog` text links on the right
- No background, no border — just a flat row aligned with the `max-w-2xl` content column
- Active link = `text-black font-medium`, inactive = `text-muted hover:text-black`
- Rendered once in `layout.tsx` above `{children}` → appears on every page automatically

### 2. Simplified Homepage (`src/app/page.tsx`)
Remove: Current Bets section, Experience section, Socials section (and their imports)
Keep: 1-sentence bio + h1, Philosophy section (with PixelPhilosophyIcon), Writing section (with PixelBlogIcon + 3 latest posts)
Change: `pt-24` → `pt-16` on `<main>` (navbar now takes up top space)

### 3. About Page (`src/app/about/page.tsx`)
New page with full CV content:
- Full bio (2 paragraphs, Vlerick mention)
- Current Bets section (PixelBetsIcon + project cards with PixelArrowIcon)
- Previous Experience section (PixelExperienceIcon + 3 jobs)
- Social links (Twitter, GitHub, LinkedIn, Mail)
- Footer

---

## File Changes

| Operation | File |
|---|---|
| Create | `src/components/Navbar.tsx` |
| Create | `src/app/about/page.tsx` |
| Modify | `src/app/layout.tsx` — import + render `<Navbar />` above `{children}` |
| Modify | `src/app/page.tsx` — strip to bio + philosophy + writing, pt-24 → pt-16 |
| Modify | `src/app/blog/page.tsx` — pt-24 → pt-16, remove top back-link |
| Modify | `src/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` — pt-24 → pt-16, remove top back-link |

`globals.css`, icon components, `posts.ts`, `next.config.ts` — no changes.

---

## Key Technical Detail

`usePathname()` requires `'use client'`. The Navbar is the only Client Component. Active link logic:
- `/` → active only when `pathname === '/'`
- `/about` → active when `pathname.startsWith('/about')`
- `/blog` → active when `pathname.startsWith('/blog')` (covers `/blog/[slug]` too)

Works fine with `output: 'export'` — hydrates in browser, no SSR issues.

---

## Verification
1. `npm run dev`
2. Home (`/`) — shows 1-sentence bio, Philosophy, Writing only. Navbar at top with "Home" highlighted.
3. About (`/about`) — shows full bio, Current Bets, Experience, socials. "About" link highlighted in nav.
4. Blog (`/blog`) — "Blog" link highlighted in nav.
5. Individual post — "Blog" link still highlighted in nav.
6. `npm run build` — clean static export, `out/about/index.html` exists.

# Personal Website: Blog + Pixel Art Upgrade

## Context
You want a personal website with a blog ("thoughts"), intro, socials, and startups — inspired by shiyankoh.com's clean minimal layout, but differentiated by pixel art icons. You already have a working Next.js 16 + React 19 + Tailwind v4 site at `/Users/canerden/personal-website` with your bio, projects, and experience. This plan extends it with a blog and pixel icon design system.

---

## What Gets Built

### 1. Blog Infrastructure
- `content/posts/*.md` — markdown files with YAML frontmatter
- `src/lib/posts.ts` — data layer (reads files, parses frontmatter, converts markdown → HTML)
- `src/app/blog/page.tsx` — full post list
- `src/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` — individual post

### 2. Pixel Art Icon Components
Five 8x8 pixel icons rendered as inline SVGs (using `<rect>` elements on a pixel grid):
- `PixelBlogIcon` — pen/document (for "Writing" section)
- `PixelBetsIcon` — rocket (for "Current Bets")
- `PixelExperienceIcon` — briefcase (for "Previous")
- `PixelPhilosophyIcon` — lightbulb (for "Philosophy")
- `PixelArrowIcon` — diagonal arrow (replaces lucide ArrowUpRight on links)

All icons use `currentColor` so they automatically inherit text color, and get `image-rendering: pixelated` for crispness.

### 3. Updated Home Page
- Section headers get inline pixel icons (12px, beside the heading text)
- New "Writing" section added before Socials — shows latest 3 posts with title, date, excerpt
- "All posts →" link to `/blog`

---

## File Structure

**New files:**
```
content/posts/
  founder-as-director.md
  why-agentic-ai-changes-gtm.md
src/
  lib/
    posts.ts
  components/
    icons/
      PixelBlogIcon.tsx
      PixelBetsIcon.tsx
      PixelExperienceIcon.tsx
      PixelPhilosophyIcon.tsx
      PixelArrowIcon.tsx
  app/
    blog/
      page.tsx
      [slug]/
        page.tsx
```

**Modified files:**
```
src/app/page.tsx          — add Writing section + pixel icons to headers
src/app/globals.css       — add .prose-content styles + .pixel-icon CSS
```

---

## Key Technical Details

### Dependencies to add
```bash
npm install gray-matter remark remark-html
```

### Post frontmatter schema
```markdown
---
title: "The Founder as Director"
date: "2026-01-15"
excerpt: "Why founders won't write code — they'll direct agents."
slug: "founder-as-director"
published: true
---
```

### Data flow (all at build time, compatible with `output: 'export'`)
`content/posts/*.md` → `fs.readFileSync` → `gray-matter` (frontmatter) → `remark-html` (body) → Server Components → static HTML in `out/`

### Critical: Next.js 16 async params
Dynamic route `params` is a Promise in Next.js 15+:
```tsx
export default async function PostPage({
  params,
}: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) {
  const { slug } = await params;
```

### Blog post prose styles
Since `@tailwindcss/typography` isn't stable for Tailwind v4, prose styles are added manually as `.prose-content` in `globals.css` (h2, p, ul, blockquote, code, etc.).

---

## Verification
1. `npm install` — confirm gray-matter, remark, remark-html added
2. `npm run dev` — home page shows pixel icons in section headers + Writing section with sample posts
3. Visit `/blog` — all posts listed chronologically
4. Visit `/blog/founder-as-director` — full post renders with prose styling
5. `npm run build` — confirm `out/blog/` and `out/blog/founder-as-director/index.html` exist (static export works)

# Personal Website: About Page + Navbar + Homepage Simplification

## Context
Homepage currently shows everything (bio, current bets, experience, philosophy, writing, socials). The goal is to strip the homepage to the essentials — 1-sentence bio, philosophy, writing — and move the full CV (bets, experience, socials) to a new `/about` page. A small minimal navbar ties all pages together.

---

## What Gets Built

### 1. Navbar (`src/components/Navbar.tsx`)
- Client Component (`'use client'`) so `usePathname()` can highlight the active link
- Layout: `Can Erden` name on the left, `Home · About · Blog` text links on the right
- No background, no border — just a flat row aligned with the `max-w-2xl` content column
- Active link = `text-black font-medium`, inactive = `text-muted hover:text-black`
- Rendered once in `layout.tsx` above `{children}` → appears on every page automatically

### 2. Simplified Homepage (`src/app/page.tsx`)
Remove: Current Bets section, Experience section, Socials section (and their imports)
Keep: 1-sentence bio + h1, Philosophy section (with PixelPhilosophyIcon), Writing section (with PixelBlogIcon + 3 latest posts)
Change: `pt-24` → `pt-16` on `<main>` (navbar now takes up top space)

### 3. About Page (`src/app/about/page.tsx`)
New page with full CV content:
- Full bio (2 paragraphs, Vlerick mention)
- Current Bets section (PixelBetsIcon + project cards with PixelArrowIcon)
- Previous Experience section (PixelExperienceIcon + 3 jobs)
- Social links (Twitter, GitHub, LinkedIn, Mail)
- Footer

---

## File Changes

| Operation | File |
|---|---|
| Create | `src/components/Navbar.tsx` |
| Create | `src/app/about/page.tsx` |
| Modify | `src/app/layout.tsx` — import + render `<Navbar />` above `{children}` |
| Modify | `src/app/page.tsx` — strip to bio + philosophy + writing, pt-24 → pt-16 |
| Modify | `src/app/blog/page.tsx` — pt-24 → pt-16, remove top back-link |
| Modify | `src/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` — pt-24 → pt-16, remove top back-link |

`globals.css`, icon components, `posts.ts`, `next.config.ts` — no changes.

---

## Key Technical Detail

`usePathname()` requires `'use client'`. The Navbar is the only Client Component. Active link logic:
- `/` → active only when `pathname === '/'`
- `/about` → active when `pathname.startsWith('/about')`
- `/blog` → active when `pathname.startsWith('/blog')` (covers `/blog/[slug]` too)

Works fine with `output: 'export'` — hydrates in browser, no SSR issues.

---

## Verification
1. `npm run dev`
2. Home (`/`) — shows 1-sentence bio, Philosophy, Writing only. Navbar at top with "Home" highlighted.
3. About (`/about`) — shows full bio, Current Bets, Experience, socials. "About" link highlighted in nav.
4. Blog (`/blog`) — "Blog" link highlighted in nav.
5. Individual post — "Blog" link still highlighted in nav.
6. `npm run build` — clean static export, `out/about/index.html` exists.
